title: How Alberta eradicated rats - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: Alberta has stayed rat-free for over seventy years by acting before rats established, sealing a single narrow entry corridor with a Rat Control Zone and an aggressive 1950s poisoning campaign. The piece is a case study in the economics of early prevention versus permanent management, and in the perpetual low-cost maintenance that keeps the win in place.

insights.claim: Acting before rats established let Alberta avoid a permanent infestation; had it waited it would now spend millions a year managing rats like its neighbors, illustrating that early eradication windows close permanently.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A transferable framework for any invasive or compounding threat: prevention has a hard expiry date after which only costly perpetual management remains.
insights.quote: Had it waited, the opportunity would have been gone for good
insights.locator: This half a million square kilometres

insights.claim: Saskatchewan spends ~C$1.2 million a year on rat control yet remains infested with over C$15 million in annual agricultural damage, while Alberta now spends only ~C$500,000 and has negligible rat damage.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the durable ROI gap between eradication-then-maintain (Alberta) and perpetual management (Saskatchewan): higher upfront spend yields far lower steady-state cost.
insights.quote: Saskatchewan spends about C$1.2 million each year on its rat control program, and yet it continues to be thoroughly infested with rats, which cause over C$15 million a year in agricultural damage.

insights.claim: Alberta accepted higher upfront cost (the initial poisoning program cost ~C$1.75 million today) and real human/animal health risk in exchange for certainty that rats would never breed.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Decisive early action carries front-loaded financial and externality costs; the bargain is paying once for certainty versus paying forever for partial control.
insights.quote: taking a preventative rather than a responsive approach meant higher upfront financial costs
insights.locator: Alberta’s early campaign used very blunt tools

insights.claim: Alberta could defend against rats cheaply because invasion was only possible through one narrow eastern corridor; mountains, cold and sparse population sealed the other borders.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Defensibility depends on funnelling a threat to a single chokepoint; the strategy is not portable to places with diffuse entry.
insights.quote: Rats could enter the province only from Saskatchewan, as Montana was too sparsely populated, the Northwest Territories were too cold, and the border with British Columbia was too mountainous.

insights.claim: Concentrating defense in a 600-by-29-km Rat Control Zone let Alberta inspect a 17,400 sq km strip instead of spreading resources over 100,000 sq km of border.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Targeting the single point of entry, not the whole perimeter, is what made surveillance economically feasible.
insights.quote: Albertan officials could target a single point of entry 17,400 square kilometers in size.
insights.locator: The Rat Control Zone made regular inspections manageable.

insights.claim: Rats' dependence on humans was turned into an advantage: harsh, sparsely settled prairie meant rats could not naturally hop between farms and instead traveled with people, making inspection of vehicles and cargo effective.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Understanding a threat's transmission vector (human-borne, not self-propagating here) lets defenders concentrate effort where it actually spreads.
insights.quote: the fact that Norway rats are so dependent on humans was an opportunity
insights.locator: In this case, the fact that Norway rats

insights.claim: Alberta built a 'rodent surveillance state' combining annual high-risk site visits, public hotlines, posters and a radio show, plus a law obliging every person and municipality to destroy rats, with fines and convictions for noncompliance.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Sustained eradication needed both proactive detection and legal coercion; voluntary cooperation alone was insufficient early on.
insights.quote: Once inspectors had been trained, officials could create what amounted to a rodent surveillance state.

insights.claim: Cold War 'infiltration from the east' rhetoric was deliberately borrowed to build public vigilance, framing rats as an invading enemy.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Public-goods campaigns can be mobilized by mapping a threat onto an existing cultural narrative to sustain citizen participation.
insights.quote: The campaign borrowed Cold War rhetoric about ‘infiltration from the east’ and the need for constant vigilance against hidden enemies.
insights.locator: The campaign borrowed Cold War rhetoric

insights.claim: The arrival of warfarin, a slow-acting anticoagulant that rats can't associate with sickness and that is far safer for humans and other animals, displaced arsenic and was decisive for sustainable long-term control.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A better technology (lower collateral harm, no bait-shyness) turned a risky blunt program into a maintainable one; tool quality gates the durability of a control regime.
insights.quote: Warfarin is a slow-acting anticoagulant which causes death by internal bleeding. This avoids the problem of rats associating the bait with sickness, so they eat until consuming a lethal dose.

insights.claim: Warfarin adoption required local adaptation and trust-building: Wisconsin's recommended corn pairing failed in Alberta, so rolled oats with 5% icing sugar was substituted, and an officer publicly ate treated oats to prove safety.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Importing a technology requires local calibration and visible trust signals to overcome adoption resistance, especially among skeptical communities.
insights.quote: a pest control officer held a series of local meetings where he ate warfarin-treated rolled oats while discussing rat control
insights.locator: But it was easy to prove warfarin was safe

insights.claim: Alberta's rat-free status is not a one-time victory but perpetual maintenance: rats still cross the border constantly and the program would fail forever if it lapsed, yet it costs each resident under C$0.11 per year.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A core insight on civilizational upkeep: durable wins are maintenance regimes whose tiny ongoing cost guards against catastrophic, irreversible reversal.
insights.quote: It’s also an example of the day-to-day maintenance that civilization is built of
insights.locator: This half a million square kilometres

insights.claim: Surveillance now mostly catches false alarms (of 875 reported sightings in 2025 only 47 were real rats) yet a single report can trigger a full investigation, showing the cost of vigilance is mostly noise but still worth paying.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Maintaining a near-zero baseline means tolerating a very low signal-to-noise ratio in monitoring as the price of preventing reestablishment.
insights.quote: of the 875 reported sightings in 2025, only 47 turned out to be actual rats
